ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific entry for fried Chinese pomfret (Pampus chinensis). FOODON_03301116 (fried fish food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pomfret (Pampus spp.) — closest available FoodEx2 species-level code for pomfret; fried preparation not separately coded at species level.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 codes pomfret at genus level (Pampus spp.) without a dedicated fried-preparation sub-code. A00LC is used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226888001 (Fried fish) is the closest available concept; no species-specific fried pomfret concept exists in SNOMED CT.

serving.description: 1 whole fish (approximately 300 g edible portion after frying)
serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is one whole fried Chinese pomfret, typically weighing approximately 300 g as served, representing a single-person main-course portion at a Singapore seafood restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish, pompano, florida, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175137) adapted for deep-fried Chinese pomfret; cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Pampus chinensis and HPB Singapore nutrient reference values for fried fish.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO-INFOODS / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for fried Chinese pomfret (Pampus chinensis). Values are adapted from USDA dry-heat cooked pompano (a comparable fatty marine fish) with upward adjustment to fat and energy to account for deep-frying oil absorption (approximately +5–8 g fat per 100 g versus dry-heat cooking), consistent with FAO/INFOODS SEA fried fish benchmarks and HPB Singapore fried fish reference ranges. Protein values are conserved from the raw/cooked fish baseline. Sodium is estimated from typical light soy sauce and salt seasoning used in Chinese-style fried fish preparations.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous estimate: protein- and fat-dominant fish dishes carry negligible glycemic response; GI assigned as effectively 0 (below measurable threshold) consistent with University of Sydney GI Database convention for pure protein/fat foods.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fried Chinese pomfret. Fried fish is a protein- and fat-dominant food with minimal carbohydrate (approximately 2 g per 100 g, largely from batter/seasoning). The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature consistently assign GI values of 0 or below measurable threshold for pure protein/fat foods. Per Veritas v2 rules for effectively zero-GI foods, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive value) and gi_category to low. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (GI 1 × carbohydrate_g_per_serving 6.0) / 100 = 0.06, rounded to 0.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and salt seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Each 300 g serving of fried Chinese pomfret contributes approximately 1260 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and limit portion size to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content and glycemic index of 1
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fried Chinese pomfret has a glycemic index of 1 (effectively zero) and only approximately 6 g of carbohydrate per 300 g serving, making it a suitable protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the high fat content from deep-frying should be considered in the context of overall caloric management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying oil absorption elevating total and saturated fat; beneficial omega-3 fatty acids partially offset risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While Chinese pomfret provides beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (approximately 3.8 g per 100 g), deep-frying significantly increases total fat and saturated fat content; individuals with cardiovascular disease should prefer steamed or grilled pomfret preparations and limit fried fish consumption to occasional servings.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Chinese Pomfret (炸斗底鲳; Zha Dou Di Chang) is a popular Chinese seafood dish served in Singapore hawker centres, seafood restaurants, and zi char stalls. The dish features a whole Chinese pomfret (Pampus chinensis), a prized silver-bodied marine fish native to the Indo-Pacific, that is seasoned with salt, light soy sauce, ginger, and garlic before being deep-fried in hot oil until the skin is golden-brown and crispy while the flesh remains moist and tender. The result is a dish with a satisfying contrast of textures: a crackling, savoury exterior and a delicate, slightly sweet, flaky white flesh interior. Chinese pomfret is considered one of the most prestigious and expensive food fish in Chinese culinary tradition, valued for its clean flavour, minimal bones, and rich omega-3 fatty acid content. Nutritionally, fried Chinese pomfret is high in protein (approximately 20.5 g per 100 g) and moderate-to-high in fat (approximately 14.8 g per 100 g) due to oil absorption during deep-frying. It provides significant amounts of vitamin B12, vitamin D, selenium, phosphorus, and niacin. The carbohydrate content is negligible, giving it a glycemic index of 1 (effectively zero), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose levels. However, the sodium content is elevated due to soy sauce seasoning, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension. The dish is typically served as a shared main course alongside steamed rice and vegetables in a Chinese family-style meal. In Singapore, it is commonly found at zi char restaurants and seafood establishments, often priced at a premium reflecting the high market value of Chinese pomfret.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fried pomfret?
Chinese pomfret vs Indian pomfret nutrition
Is fried pomfret good for heart health?
meal_context: Shared main course with steamed rice

confidence_reason: No exact entry for fried Chinese pomfret (Pampus chinensis) exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA at the species-and-preparation level. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA dry-heat cooked pompano (a comparable fatty marine fish) with adjustments for deep-frying oil absorption, cross-referenced against FAO/INFOODS SEA fried fish benchmarks. Confidence is medium rather than high because the adaptation involves estimation rather than direct measurement of this specific dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central (dry-heat cooked pompano, FDC ID 175137) adapted for deep-frying, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA fried fish data and HPB Singapore fried fish reference ranges. No exact species-and-preparation match was available in any approved primary source. 2. dietary_fibre_g is set to 0.0 as fried fish contains no dietary fibre; this is confirmed zero per USDA and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for fried fish. 3. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as fried Chinese pomfret is a protein- and fat-dominant food with negligible carbohydrate content (2.0g per 100g); GI is effectively zero per University of Sydney GI Database convention for pure protein/fat foods, and set to 1 as the lowest valid positive value as required by Veritas v2 rules; gi_category is low. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.1, calculated as (GI 1 * carbohydrate_g_per_serving 6.0) / 100 = 0.06, rounded to 0.1. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from the adapted USDA pompano baseline and are not directly measured for this specific dish. 5. The food name in the input JSON uses '昌' (GD001797: 炸斗底昌) which appears to be a variant or informal character for '鲳' (pomfret); the canonical Chinese name 炸斗底鲳/炸斗底鯧 is used in this record. 6. Halal status is assumed based on the fish-only protein source and typical zi char preparation; individual restaurant practices may vary. 7. FoodEx2 code A00LC is used as the closest defensible anchor for pomfret (Pampus spp.); no fried-preparation sub-code exists at species level in FoodEx2. 8. All -1 sentinel values in per_100g represent nutrients that are estimated from the adapted source rather than directly reported; they are populated with estimated values in this record and no -1 values remain in the final output.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built complete record from scratch. Adapted nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central pompano (FDC ID 175137) with deep-frying adjustment. Added all mandatory Singaporean multilingual names (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Populated all ontology fields with closest_parent codes and gap notes. Wrote 1,050-character LLM description. Added all three health context conditions with clinical guidance. Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency. Set glycemic_index to 0 with gi_category low and documented analogous estimate basis. Confirmed all per_100g values are numeric with no -1 sentinel values remaining. Record failed due to glycemic_index being 0 (q4 failure).

Iteration 2: Corrected glycemic_index from 0 to 1 as required by Veritas v2 rules for effectively zero-GI foods. Recalculated glycemic_load_per_serving to 0.1 based on GI=1 and 6g carbohydrate per serving. Updated gi_source.source_match_notes, health_context.type2_diabetes.guidance, llm_training.natural_language_description, and notes to reflect the GI=1 change and GL calculation. This resolved the q4 failure.
